summary: Magical second‑set jam marathon; Scarlet Begonias shines
blurb: The Detroit show delivers a jaw‑dropping Wheel > Good Lovin' > Comes A Time sequence and a soaring Playing In The Band. Scarlet Begonias kicks off the first set with classic Garcia riffs, while the second half rides a wave of deep group interplay. Overall a strong performance despite an uneven opening.
review: On October 3, 1976 at Cobo Arena the Dead turned a shaky first set into one of their most rewarding second‑set marathons of the year. Early on the band stumbled through a thin‑sounding Loser and El Paso, but the magic ignited with Scarlet Begonias – Jerry’s lead work is described as “sweet” and “ripping”, while Phil’s bass underpins the groove. The real highlight unfurls after the drum break: Playing In The Band flows into a thunderous Wheel, segues through an expansive jam, then erupts into Good Lovin' and culminates in a soaring Comes A Time that several reviewers rank among the top‑50 jams of any era. Keith’s piano before Comes A Time adds a “Take Five”‑like texture, and Mickey and Bill drive a “get‑up‑and‑dance” force that propels the band forward. Though the first half shows occasional thin guitar tones and an over‑prominent keyboard mix on the Miller SBD, the audience tapes capture the room’s energy and make the second set feel immersive. In short, this show is a must‑listen for anyone chasing that classic ’76 second‑set bliss.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are flagged positive and two mixed; all praise the Wheel > Good Lovin' > Comes A Time stretch, Scarlet Begonias, and Playing In The Band while noting only minor first‑set flaws. Consistent enthusiasm across sources yields a strong positive sentiment adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Fair‑quality soundboard capture that balances the full show, with a noticeably better mix on the second set where the key jams reside.

key_highlights: Wheel > Good Lovin' > Comes A Time sequence ranks among the band’s top jams
Scarlet Begonias opens the first set with classic Garcia riffs
Playing In The Band segues into a marathon of drums, Wheel and jam sections
Keith’s piano before Comes A Time adds a jazzy “Take Five” flavor
song_highlights: It Must Have Been The Roses

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers sweet, expressive lead work and rips through Scarlet Begonias; his solos on the second‑set jams are described as immense fun and relentless.
band_performance.Phil: Provides strong rhythmic leadership, especially a bass takeover at the end of Good Lovin'; Phil‑centric moments are highlighted throughout the jam stretches.
band_performance.Bob: Adds vocal‑driven flair to Good Lovin' and teases China Cat Sunflower; his guitar occasionally recedes early but reappears with key chord work later.
band_performance.Keith: Keyboard mix can dominate on the Miller SBD, yet his piano before Comes A Time earns praise for a jazzy texture that weaves through the jam.
band_performance.Mickey: Drumming described as a get‑up‑and‑dance force, driving the band forward especially during the Wheel and drum‑jam segment.
band_performance.Billy: Partners Mickey in a Zep/Deep Purple‑style power drive; his contributions are central to the high‑energy second set.

must_listen_sequences: It Must Have Been The Roses
